nodes.label: Thermic effect of food and metabolic cost
Eating rate, texture, chewing, and sensory exposure
Reward, palatability, dopamine, and food choice
Calorie measurement, Atwater factors, and labeling error
Set point, settling point, and body weight regulation
Leptin, adipose signaling, and endocrine feedback

claims.claim: Calorie counts are useful but incomplete because they omit biological context.
The same calorie count can produce different biological events depending on form, speed, matrix, protein, fiber, and processing.
Food form, texture, liquid/solid state, and eating rate can alter satiety and subsequent intake.
Ultra-processed foods can promote passive overconsumption through mechanisms beyond stated calories alone.
Protein calories can differ from other calories in satiety signaling and thermic cost.
Fiber and intact food structure can alter metabolizable energy, gastric emptying, microbiome fermentation, and satiety.
Glycemic and insulin responses vary by person, food matrix, sequence, timing, and context.
Energy expenditure can adapt or compensate, so intake/expenditure arithmetic is not always behaviorally simple.
Calorie measurement and labeling systems involve assumptions, estimates, and tolerances.
Energy balance remains physically true and is the fair constraint on any calories-not-equal argument.

entries.title: Marked changes in one-carbon metabolism on a low-carbohydrate high-fat diet: a randomized controlled trial (CARBFUNC).
entries.summary: Studying the impact of dietary carbohydrates and fat on markers of one-carbon metabolism could provide important insight into distinct metabolic adaptations and how diets affects disease risk. entries.title: Eating rate has sustained effects on energy intake from ultraprocessed diets: a 2-week ad libitum dietary randomized controlled crossover trial.
entries.journal: The American journal of clinical nutrition
entries.summary: Previous research has shown that diets dominated by ultraprocessed foods (UPF) are associated with higher dietary energy intakes. This association may be attributable in part to meal texture and associated eating rate (ER). Experimental studies are needed to clarify the underlying mechanisms. The objective was to compare daily energy intake (kcal/d) from diets with different meal texture-derived ER (g/min) (UPF Slow-ER compared to UPF Fast-ER) across a 14-day period. Two UPF diets comprised of f…

entries.title: Impact of food texture and degree of food processing on post-meal satiety and later snack intake.
entries.summary: Ultra-processed-foods are speculated offer lower satiety than the same energy consumed in less-processed forms. Meal-texture influences eating rate (ER) and energy intake within a meal, but it remains unclear whether these texture effects influence post-meal experience of satiety in minimally and highly processed meals. We examined whether food texture (softer vs. harder) influence post-meal satiety in minimally- and ultra-processed fixed portion meals. Across four test-sessions in a randomised …

entries.title: Individual glycemic responses scale predictably with the glycemic index: evidence against person-specific food effects.
entries.journal: The American journal of clinical nutrition
entries.summary: Recent personalized nutrition research has reported large inter-individual differences in postprandial glucose responses to identical foods, raising questions about whether these differences reflect food-specific personal effects or normal day-to-day variability in glucose tolerance. entries.title: Constant-routine protocol reveals an endogenous circadian rhythm in diet-induced thermogenesis with a peak in the biological morning.
entries.summary: Late meal timing has been linked to increased body mass, although the physiological mechanisms are unknown. While previous research has suggested that identical meals consumed in the morning produce greater thermogenic responses than evening meals, it is unclear whether this is due to endogenous circadian control or due to behavioral and environmental factors. Therefore, this mechanistic randomized trial tested whether the endogenous circadian system, thus independently of sleep/wake, rest/activ…
